comments:
Summary:
This paper addresses Machine Reading at Scale (MRS): answering open-domain factoid questions using Wikipedia as the sole knowledge source. The authors present DrQA, a two-stage system with (1) Document Retriever based on TF–IDF plus bigram hashing, and (2) Document Reader, a 3-layer bidirectional LSTM span-extraction model enriched with exact-match, POS/NER/TF features, and an aligned question embedding. They train on SQuAD and augment training with distantly supervised data from other QA collections, showing that multitask learning and DS improve performance. Document Retriever outperforms the Wikipedia search API and Document Reader achieves state-of-the-art on SQuAD; the end-to-end DrQA system gives competitive results on multiple open-domain QA datasets.

Strengths:
- Clearly motivated task (MRS) that combines realistic large-scale retrieval with machine comprehension. The separation of retrieval and reading is well justified.
- Simple, efficient, and effective Document Retriever (bigram hashing + TF–IDF) that empirically beats the built-in Wikipedia search.
- Strong Document Reader architecture with useful feature engineering (exact match, aligned question embedding) and solid empirical results (top SQuAD performance reported here).
- Thorough empirical evaluation across multiple datasets and experiment settings (SQuAD-only, fine-tune with DS, multitask DS), showing benefits of additional DS data and multitask learning.
- Honest analysis of the large performance drop from paragraph-level to full-Wikipedia QA and discussion of causes (retrieval, question ambiguity).

Weaknesses / Points for improvement:
- The DS procedure, while practical, may introduce noisy labels; more analysis quantifying noise in DS examples and its effect on reader training would be valuable.
- The retrieval and reading components are trained independently; while acknowledged in the paper, empirical exploration of joint or end-to-end training is not provided. This is a promising direction to close the gap between paragraph-level and full-Wikipedia performance.
- Aggregation across multiple paragraphs/documents is handled at prediction time (argmax over spans). Training the reader to jointly consider multiple paragraphs (or incorporate a document-level attention/ranking objective) could improve robustness, but such experiments are not included.
- Some implementation details and resource requirements are missing or sparse (e.g., runtime, memory footprint for indexing and retrieval over ~5M articles, exact training data sizes after DS filtering). Providing these would help reproducibility and practical assessment.
- Evaluation uses exact-match against gold spans; for some datasets (e.g., WebQuestions) canonicalization or relaxed matching might be needed — more discussion of evaluation caveats would help.

Recommendations / Suggestions:
- Include a more detailed analysis of DS quality: fraction of noisy pairs, precision of paragraph selection heuristics, and the effect of filtering thresholds.
- Experiment with document-level models or joint retrieval–reading training to show if the retrieval–reader pipeline can be improved end-to-end.
- Provide runtime/memory benchmarks for indexing, retrieval latency, and reader throughput; release code and the retrieval index if possible to facilitate reproduction.
- Consider richer candidate aggregation strategies (confidence calibration across paragraphs, reranking answers by document-level features) and report ablations.

Conclusion:
This paper makes a significant contribution by framing and empirically tackling machine reading at scale with a simple, effective, and well-evaluated system. The experiments are comprehensive and the analysis honest about limitations. I recommend acceptance; the work will be of high interest to researchers working on open-domain QA and machine comprehension.
